content.problemStatement:
Chef has a grid of size N×MN \times MN×M.

In one move, Chef can move from his current cell to an adjacent cell. If the Chef is currently at (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) then, in one move, Chef can move to (i+1,j),(i−1,j),(i,j+1)(i + 1, j), (i - 1, j), (i, j + 1)(i+1,j),(i−1,j),(i,j+1) or (i,j−1)(i, j - 1)(i,j−1).

There are also KKK special cells in the grid. These special cells support a corner move as well. If the Chef is currently at a special cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) then, in one move, Chef can move to (i+1,j+1),(i−1,j−1),(i+1,j−1)(i + 1, j + 1), (i - 1, j - 1), (i + 1, j - 1)(i+1,j+1),(i−1,j−1),(i+1,j−1) or (i−1,j+1)(i - 1, j + 1)(i−1,j+1) in addition to the adjacent cells ((i+1,j),(i−1,j),(i,j+1)(i + 1, j), (i - 1, j), (i, j + 1)(i+1,j),(i−1,j),(i,j+1) or (i,j−1)(i, j - 1)(i,j−1)).

Chef is not allowed to leave the grid at any moment. Determine the **minimum** number of moves Chef needs to go from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) to (N,M)(N, M)(N,M).


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains three space-separated integers NNN, MMM and KKK — the number of rows in the grid, the number of columns in the grid and the number of special cells.
KKK lines follow. The iii-th line of these KKK lines contains two space-separated integers XiX_iXi​ and YiY_iYi​ such that (Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i)(Xi​,Yi​) is the iii-th special cell.


Output Format
For each test case, output the minimum number of moves required by the Chef to go to (N,M)(N, M)(N,M) from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1).
constraints: Sum of K over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case starts with three integers N, M and K – the number of rows, columns and special cells. The next K lines each contain two integers Xi Yi describing a special cell (Xi, Yi).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of moves required for Chef to travel from (1,1) to (N,M).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1: There is no special cell in this case. One of the optimal ways is: (1,1)→(1,2)→(1,3)→(2,3)→(3,3)(1,1) \rightarrow (1,2) \rightarrow (1,3) \rightarrow (2,3) \rightarrow (3,3)(1,1)→(1,2)→(1,3)→(2,3)→(3,3). Therefore a total of 444 moves is required.
Test Case 2: There are 222 special cells in this case. One of the optimal ways is: (1,1)→(2,1)→(2,2)→(3,3)(1,1) \rightarrow (2,1) \rightarrow (2,2) \rightarrow (3,3)(1,1)→(2,1)→(2,2)→(3,3). Therefore a total of 333 moves is required.

solution.approach:
Moving orthogonally (up, down, left, right) always costs one move. A diagonal move (down‑right) replaces two orthogonal moves, therefore each diagonal saves exactly one step. Diagonal moves are allowed only when Chef starts them from a special cell. Consequently the problem becomes: maximise the number of down‑right diagonal moves that can be performed on a monotone path from (1,1) to (N,M). After a diagonal from (x,y) Chef lands at (x+1,y+1). From there he can walk orthogonally to any cell with coordinates ≥ (x+1, y+1). Hence we only need a sequence of special cells (x1,y1), (x2,y2), … such that 1 ≤ x1 < x2 < … < N and 1 ≤ y1 < y2 < … < M. The longest possible number of diagonals equals the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence (LIS) in two dimensions among the special cells that lie strictly inside the grid (x < N and y < M). The answer is (N‑1)+(M‑1) – LIS_length.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Read N, M, K.
2. Keep only specials with x < N and y < M.
3. Sort the remaining specials by x ascending, and for equal x by y ascending.
4. Run a classic O(K log K) LIS on the y‑coordinates using a vector `dp` where `dp[i]` is the smallest possible tail y of an increasing subsequence of length i+1. For each y, find the lower_bound position in `dp` and replace it (lower_bound gives the first element ≥ y, guaranteeing strict increase).
5. Let L = dp.size() (the LIS length). Output (N+M‑2) – L.

Correctness follows from the observation that any feasible path can be transformed into a monotone path that uses only down‑right diagonals, and the set of starting cells of those diagonals must be a strictly increasing chain. Conversely, any such chain can be realised by walking orthogonally between consecutive specials and taking a diagonal at each of them.

Complexities: Sorting O(K log K) and LIS O(K log K). Memory O(K).
solution.timeComplexity: O(K log K) per test case (K is the number of special cells in that test).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(K) for storing the filtered specials and the LIS helper array.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(t):
        N = int(next(it)); M = int(next(it)); K = int(next(it))
        specials = []
        for __ in range(K):
            x = int(next(it)); y = int(next(it))
            if x < N and y < M:
                specials.append((x, y))
        specials.sort(key=lambda p: (p[0], p[1]))
        dp = []
        import bisect
        for _, y in specials:
            pos = bisect.bisect_left(dp, y)
            if pos == len(dp):
                dp.append(y)
            else:
                dp[pos] = y
        ans = (N - 1) + (M - 1) - len(dp)
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
